nativeRange.habitat: Hard-bottom reef habitats; typically found attached to limestone, rubble, or rocky substrate in high-current zones

avoidWith: herbivorous fish that may graze heavily on macroalgae

imageCandidates.notes: Product photo showing iridescent branching thallus with blue structural coloration under reef lighting.

summary: A compact Caribbean red macroalgae with a striking structural iridescence — the blue-green shimmer is produced by light refraction off the thallus surface, not pigment. Generally suited to display placement in established reef tanks.
careNotes:
Color-Shifting Laurencia is a specialty red macroalgae native to Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico hard-bottom reef habitats, where it grows attached to limestone, rubble, and rocky substrate in current-swept zones. It is sold in the aquarium trade under the scientific name Laurencia iridescens, though the accepted name in current taxonomy is Yuzurua iridescens (Sentíes & M.J.Wynne, 2015), in the family Rhodomelaceae.

Its most striking feature is a brilliant blue-to-green iridescence that becomes visible under reef lighting. This coloration is structural in origin — produced by light interference and refraction off the microstructure of the thallus surface, not by photosynthetic or accessory pigments. Specimens may arrive looking duller after the stress of shipping but generally tend to recover their iridescent glow within a few days under stable reef conditions.

The thallus forms compact, textured clumps of rounded, club-shaped branchlets, typically reaching 4–8 cm in diameter. In the aquarium, the species generally requires secure anchoring to hard substrate — keeping the holdfast off the sandbed tends to support long-term survival. Moderate to high, turbulent flow has been reported to improve growth and longevity; stagnant conditions are best avoided.

Color-Shifting Laurencia is available from a small number of specialty online macroalgae retailers and tends to appear seasonally, as it is typically hand-collected in late spring to summer. It is considered intermediate in care — less forgiving than common refugium species such as Chaetomorpha, but not as demanding as calcareous green macroalgae. Nutrient uptake is generally considered low compared to refugium-grade species; it is kept primarily as an ornamental display specimen rather than as a nitrate or phosphate exporter.

Attachment method is important: as a red macroalgae, this species will MELT at the base if buried in a sandbed. It should be secured to live rock or hard substrate using superglue gel, zip-tie or fishing line, or wedging into a crevice. Propagation is typically achieved by fragmentation — separating a portion of the thallus with an intact holdfast base.

primary.name: Mosaic Macros
primary.notes: Primary and sole specialty hobby source for this species. Product page confirms structural iridescence, branching morphology, and Laurencia iridescens scientific name.

additional.name: Gulf Coast Ecosystems (Iridescent Candy Algae)
additional.notes: Secondary hobby source. Cross-checked sizing (1.5–3 in = ~4–8 cm), lighting (moderate), flow (moderate-high), structural color description, and Gulf/Atlantic native range.

additional.name: TankFacts – Laurencia iridescens
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 76–80°F (24–27°C), salinity 1.023–1.026 SG, pH 7.8–8.4. Partial entry but confirmed key parameters.

additional.name: WoRMS – Laurencia iridescens (AphiaID 496287)
additional.notes: Confirmed family Rhodomelaceae, order Ceramiales. Notes accepted name is Yuzurua iridescens (Sentíes & M.J.Wynne, 2015); Laurencia iridescens is the original basionym used in the aquarium trade.

additional.name: Mosaic Macros general macroalgae care guide
additional.notes: Slice-wide secondary source — provides authoritative Mosaic-wide parameter targets (temp 22-26°C, pH 8.1-8.4, Ca 400-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm, NO3 5-20 ppm, PO4 0.02-0.1 ppm) and the 4-method propagation standard. Added retroactively 2026-06-08.
